data.title: Q3 GDP Surprises, But Investments Vanished
data.description:
After a tumultuous Q1 GDP read that was negative back in April (largely caused by businesses importing like crazy) and a bounce-back in Q2, it was up in the air how Q3 was going to go. I personally believed that we would continue the trend up, but I didn't expect the result we got: 4.3% Real GDP growth.

This was a surprise report, coming in higher than most estimates. The Atlanta Fed's GDPNow, which overestimated but directionally predicted the negative read in Q1, has us on track for close to 3.5%. Bloomberg reported that this result was higher than all but one of the predictions they aggregated.

Meanwhile, while we grow at unexpected rates, consumer sentiment is near historic lows. There is a disconnect here that is worth addressing.

Spending Is Still in Focus

The U.S. has a consumption-based economy, with consumer spending making up nearly 70% of GDP. So even when I blame business imports for the first-quarter contraction, that was only part of the story. We also saw a fall in consumer spending. As we rose in Q2 and now Q3, we've seen a return of that same consumer spending.

If you listen to most people in America talk about their spending habits and prices, it may not feel like people are increasing their spending, but that's what the data is showing. This brings us back to consumer sentiment. How can it be low while consumers are increasing their spending?

One helpful piece of perspective is this chart, showing the breakdown of consumer spending by income band. Not all consumers are equals when it comes to GDP.

So if you're not in the top 10% of earners, you could be pulling back your spending quite hard but not dent GDP all that much. The top 10% of earners, in contrast to everyone else, are doing pretty well right now in the real economy, as evidenced by their return to spending in Q2 and Q3 of this year.

The average person? Last month, their real wages went negative.

The question also should be about what consumers are ramping up their spending on. In the government report on Q3 GDP from the Bureau of Economic Analysis ("BEA"), they write:

The increase in consumer spending reflected increases in both services and goods. Within services, the leading contributors were health care and other services. Within goods, the leading contributors were recreational goods and vehicles as well as other nondurable goods.

Within health care, both outpatient services as well as hospital and nursing home services increased...

Within other services, the leading contributors to the increase were international travel...as well as professional and other services (mainly legal services)...

The increase in other nondurable goods was mainly in prescription drugs...

This brings us back full circle to why the average American doesn't report good consumer health. Spending is increasing in both goods and services, but the primary rising costs are healthcare, vehicles, and prescriptions. Recreational goods also contributed, but some of these are non-negotiable spending for most. Healthcare spending is often not something people can modulate and adapt to as their financial conditions change—they just have to go to the doctor when they have to go, and the same with prescription drugs. It's unlikely that increased spending in those categories (outside recreational goods) is driven by a healthy increase in demand but instead by higher prices.

That's not the kind of GDP growth we want, although it is inevitable in some ways because healthcare companies are held to account by shareholders to continually grow their profits as opposed to how well they take care of the average American's wallet.

The Tariff Bill Coming Due?

I've been skeptical about how much impact tariff policy will have, mostly because the worst of the promises made on Liberation Day back in April were clawed back, used as a tactic to threaten foreign nations and force them to negotiate, etc.—enough geopolitics for now. Notably, many of the initial rates did not stick, and shifting policy—there have been 500+ tariff announcements since then—has created a thick web that is difficult to navigate. How it will ultimately affect the economy is highly disputed (no matter what I say here, I will be called a partisan hack by one side of the aisle; that is as delicate as I could put it), but here is one way in which they may be showing up in the data.

I suspect that tariff-related price hikes are showing up in goods pricing, which is far more heavily weighted in GDPPI. If that is running hotter than PCE, it means that goods spending is rising faster than services spending. Since we know that recreational goods were a large part of this trend, that seems to check out with me—as recreational goods as a category are more exposed to imports than vehicles or healthcare, the other major increasing categories.

This will be a trend to watch moving forward to see if this flip above PCE is temporary or part of a larger move that will play out in Q4 as well.

Investment Vanished

When we break down the components of GDP, we see the large contribution from consumption, and we can see that imports have decreased, but what was surprising to me was to see private investment drop flat to negative.

This is contrary to the narrative around AI CapEx building out the economy, but perhaps spending is "lumpier" than initially thought. Much of the downward move in investments was also caused by drops in residential.

This initial read gives me hope for a solid final read of Q3, which we will get in late January; it is delayed due to the government shutdown, but BEA data seems more complete than BLS data and unlikely to see as large of swings and revisions later from my view.

Conclusion

Despite consumer sentiment being very low, the economy ran along just fine in Q3 and rose at the fastest rate in two years, primarily led by consumer spending. While investments went flat, to my surprise, we will need to wait for the final read of Q3 and for the end-of-year Q4 tallies before we make any judgments about if business investment is slowing down meaningfully. For now, it is a yellow flag to watch, but I am still bullish on the economy and markets going into 2026.

This print came with several important data points to consider and a few reasons why the consumer sentiment report may not be misleading about how the average person feels but is misleading about where consumer spending is headed.

On the average consumer, I am less bullish, especially in regard to the unemployment rate hitting fresh highs recently. That is a pain point that is much harder to "wait and see" about than private investment levels.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: ServiceNow: Overpaying For Armis And No Synergies In Sight
data.description:
Amid a buoyant stock market in 2025, dealmaking has also been white-hot over the past year, especially in the enterprise software sector as large acquirers have taken advantage of sliding share prices to pick up recurring-revenue businesses at a great price.

We aren't surprised, however, when ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW) paid a heady >20x revenue multiple for a cybersecurity company, Armis. This move represents ServiceNow's first foray into the cybersecurity territory, and in. my view is yet another indication that ServiceNow is deploying a "growth at all costs" mindset without much regard to expanding its bottom line.

Shares of ServiceNow are down ~25% since the start of the year, and in my view will continue sliding well into 2026.

I last wrote a Sell article on ServiceNow in November, when the stock was trading at roughly $850 per share (or ~$170 adjusted for its recent 5:1 split). Since then, shares have dropped further, both on general backlash against enterprise software names as well as investors' disappointment over the Armis deal. Though valuation multiples (my chief gripe against ServiceNow stock) have compressed somewhat over the past month, the acquisition increases risk for the company, and I'm reiterating my Sell rating on this name.

A cybersecurity company that doesn't fit neatly into ServiceNow's portfolio

Let's first cover exactly what Armis does. Armis is a threat intelligence and detection platform. Its core product is called Armis Centric, which leverages an AI-driven "Asset Intelligence Engine" to discover, monitor and secure every device linked to a corporate network, scanning for threats, enforcing security protocols, and eliminating risks.

Armis is well-regarded in the industry. Gartner, often considered the top software industry reviewer, placed Armis in the Leaders category of its Magic Quadrant for CPS (Cyber-Physical Systems) Protection Platforms, with other well-known competitors including Forescout, Tenable (TENB), Fortinet, and Palo Alto Networks (PANW).

In a press release announcing the deal, ServiceNow wrote as follows justifying the rationale behind the acquisition:

Armis will expand ServiceNow’s security workflow offerings, provide leadership in cyber-physical security for critical infrastructure (OT, IoT, Cloud, and IoMT), and advance AI-native, proactive cybersecurity and

vulnerability response across all connected devices. Together, ServiceNow and Armis will connect real-time asset discovery, threat intelligence, and risk prioritization with automated remediation and response workflows to accelerate security outcomes and strengthen security posture through continuous risk reduction."

We'll state the obvious here: ServiceNow has minimal exposure to cybersecurity today. As a reminder for investors who are newer to ServiceNow, the company is best known for its IT service management tools: i.e., a central platform for business users to log IT tickets, and for IT professionals to handle and resolve jobs. Other core ServiceNow applications include customer service management, field service tools, and CPQ (configure, price and quote) applications). Existing ServiceNow security products primarily converge around the company's SecOps (security operations) tools which help companies to coordinate response to a potential cyber threat.

From a product overlap perspective, this is positive. ServiceNow is acquiring a company that brings revenue that, in my view, the company wouldn't otherwise have achieved. That said, I also think there will be few cross-selling synergies. Different business teams are in charge of buying workflow and customer management tools than those who are tasked with cybersecurity and threat intelligence; as such, I think cross-sell opportunities will still require two different sales teams.

Valuation multiples are overripe

Now let's dig into the deal economics. ServiceNow is paying $7.75 billion in all cash for Armis, for a company that is purportedly doing $340 million in ARR, at a ~50% y/y growth pace.

This means that ServiceNow is paying a 23x ARR multiple for Armis. Sure, 50% growth is fierce and indicates that Armis may quickly grow into its multiple, but we also can't forget how crowded the cybersecurity space is, and there's no guarantee that Armis can keep growing at this rate: especially when deep-pocketed rivals like Palo Alto Networks (PANW) are also spending gobs of cash on their own M&A to try to fill out their product portfolios.

Beyond the headline multiple, I have two additional concerns on the deal. The first is that ServiceNow is essentially dumping all of its net cash into this deal. ServiceNow's balance sheet as of the most recent third quarter held $9.68 billion of cash and $1.49 billion of debt, or $8.19 billion of net cash. ServiceNow is essentially dumping all of its eggs into Armis, and I'm not certain that cybersecurity is the company's true area of competitive advantage in which it should be investing heavily in. We note as well that this isn't ServiceNow's only major deal of 2025; earlier this year the company also spent $2.9 billion to acquire Moveworks, an agentic AI platform.

My second concern is that a deal of this magnitude usually comes with promises of cost synergies, or at least a note on when the acquisition is meant to be earnings-accretive. The Armis deal announcement contains neither. In my view, this only serves to accentuate one of my core issues with ServiceNow: its "growth at all costs" mindset.

I continue to be concerned that, in an enterprise software industry where many rivals have shifted their focus to profit expansion (even Salesforce (CRM), famously a growth-at-all-costs company before, has now had multiple rounds of layoffs as CEO Marc Benioff turns his focus away from growth and to GAAP profits), ServiceNow has made no major strides toward efficiency - which isn't really credible when the company itself is touting agentic AI and automation for its customers. ServiceNow has not had any major companywide layoffs. Its headcount grew to 28k employees as of the end of the most recent quarter. Armis has about 950 employees, and based on the deal announcement it's highly likely that the entire team will come over to ServiceNow intact.

In other words, I expect Armis to be dilutive to ServiceNow's profit margin expansion, and make the company look more unattractive relative to software industry rivals that have shifted to a profit and efficiency-first mindset.

Key takeaways

My biggest issue with ServiceNow as an investment is its heady valuation multiples. Even after its recent correction, ServiceNow still trades at an expensive ~12x forward revenue multiple, which indexes on the high end of a set of peer software companies that are also growing in the high teens/low 20s range:

Meanwhile, the Armis deal at a ~23x ARR multiple will have the impact of shrinking ServiceNow's cash balance, further extending already expensive multiples and possibly delaying ServiceNow's profit expansion.

In my view, ServiceNow remains a bad bet, and the stock's ~3% slide on news of the Armis deal is a signal that the stock market is quite skeptical as well. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: Developing The 6G Infrastructure Could Be Crucial For AI In 2026
data.description:
Introduction

Although data center infrastructure was the dominant topic regarding AI in 2025, I think 6G infrastructure and its expansion in 2026 and beyond could become the dominant trend. Edge AI is also likely to become a larger focus, as will interconnects.

6G Infrastructure

There have already been several bottlenecks in the past that have slowed down the AI revolution. These have included shortages of GPUs, RAM, and energy. One bottleneck that does not receive much attention is that the 5G network is not designed for AI traffic.

Unlike previous network evolutions, the development and deployment of 6G will fundamentally redefine the architecture and functionality of global communications networks to go beyond connectivity. For the first time, AI will enable these networks to sense and infer at the edge, serving hundreds of billions of AI-powered endpoints and supporting AI-driven applications such as autonomous vehicles, precision agriculture and advanced manufacturing.

Source: Nvidia

In the past, telecommunications networks were designed primarily for transmitting text, voice, and video. Now, however, there is a great need to send large amounts of data and communicate with AI services. In the future, many devices will communicate with each other and collect data that must all be evaluated in some way. Qualcomm (QCOM), for example, assumes that there will be much more uplink traffic than downlink traffic in the future. The problem is that even today, Real-Time and See-What-I-See applications can overwhelm the capacity of 5G networks with just a few users.

When it comes to AI infrastructure, the data center infrastructure sector is usually mentioned and given a lot of importance. But I think 6G infrastructure will be equally important. The areas of airborne and spaceborne satellites, as well as radio access networks, will most likely produce a few companies that will experience explosive growth rates. One company that I find interesting in the satellite industry and have recently written about is Rocket Lab (RKLB).

From Cloud AI to Edge AI and Data Quality

Most of today's LLMs are based on data from the internet or textbooks. However, the knowledge that really gives companies a competitive advantage is usually found within those companies themselves. Understandably, these companies are reluctant to upload their knowledge to the cloud for training LLMs, which could ultimately benefit their competitors. This means that high-quality data will become very valuable, and specialised models for specific areas will probably be very successful in the next few years.

In terms of the speed at which data is processed, cloud-based artificial intelligence is also quickly approaching its limits – especially when it comes to advanced AI applications. Here too, edge AI promises a brighter future, characterized by faster data processing and greatly reduced latency times.

Source: Fraunhofer Institute

With Edge AI, companies no longer need to upload their data to the cloud and can also run much more efficient models, as data processing is faster. For this reason, I believe that Edge AI will receive more attention in 2026, as it will be the area from which companies can benefit greatly.

Is Alphabet revolutionizing the AI landscape once again?

I think it's well known that the Transformer architecture, on which today's LLMs are based, originated in the Google Labs. With Titans and Miras, it seems that Alphabet (GOOG) has achieved another masterpiece that has not yet received the attention it deserves. It seems that Alphabet has succeeded in giving LLMs a long-term memory that works quickly and effectively without the need for special offset retraining. So ideally, we talk about something in a chat, and Gemini incorporates this information in a different chat. This could take the quality of LLMs to a whole new level.

Since Alphabet is investing heavily in AI technology and producing outstanding results simultaneously, I believe the company is still undervalued.

Networking and AI

As AI factories scale up, achieving efficient, highly synchronized, and scalable infrastructure requires more than raw computing power - it demands advanced high-speed interconnects and optimized network topologies tailored to different traffic patterns and application stages.

Source: Gigabyte

The interaction and data exchange between the GPUs and memory should be as fast and error-free as possible. Until recently, memory was a bottleneck, now, data transmission is the new bottleneck, and silicon photonics or co-optics solutions are being tested to overcome today's limits.

It’s not about who has the best accelerator but who also has the best connectivity solutions.

Source: Marvell

I can fully agree with this statement from the Marvell Technology (MRVL) blog. But I don't believe Marvell is the company I prefer in this area. Although I published an Marvell article with a buy rating in November, it seems that Alchip is overtaking them.

GPUs and ASICs

I am still invested in Nvidia (NVIDIA) and AMD (AMD), but I believe that other companies will generate better returns in 2026. Alphabet has achieved incredible results with its TPUs and has demonstrated that Nvidia's market dominance can be challenged. So I would argue that Nvidia's competitive advantage has declined in recent months rather than increased.

One company that I predict will have a good year in 2026 is TSMC (TSM). Regardless of who emerges as the winner, TSMC will produce the chips. If TSMC were an American company, I believe it would be the world's most valuable company. However, as it is based in Taiwan, there is a geopolitical discount.

Memory

The memory market's supply situation is tighter than the GPU market, which allows SK Hynix (HXSC.F), Samsung (SSNLF), and Micron (MU) to currently sell all of their capacities. Depending on how quickly they can increase their capacity, this could lead to continued strong sales growth. However, I believe that the market has already priced this in, as evidenced by the significant rise in share prices in recent times.

Agentic AI and Robotics

I think that 2026 is still a bit too early for robotics and genuinely useful applications of agentic AI. Although there will be huge progress in both areas, I think the timeline should be more like 2027+.

Conclusion

All in all, I can say that TSMC and Alphabet are among my top five positions. So I still see strong upside potential for both. In addition, I will focus on the 6G infrastructure sector in 2026, in the hope of identifying the companies that will benefit from it. Another company that I find very interesting and that I believe has a lot of potential is Nebius (NBIS). I wrote an article about this company a few days ago, in which I emphasised its favourable valuation.

data.title: Harbor SMID Cap Value ETF Q3 2025 Portfolio Update
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Harbor SMID Cap Value ETF Q3 2025 Commentary.

Contributors and Detractors

Detracting from performance was Synopsys (SNPS), a global leader in electronic design automation, the specialized software used by engineers to design and test complex semiconductor chips before they are manufactured. The company also provides semiconductor intellectual property through its DesignWare portfolio, which includes pre-built components such as high-speed interfaces, processors, and security modules that help customers accelerate development and reduce design risk.

During the period, shares traded lower by 15% as softer results in the intellectual property (“IP”) segment weighed on performance. Revenue was pressured by temporary disruption from U.S. export restrictions on China and delays tied to Intel’s new foundry launch. We believe these represent timing issues rather than lasting demand weakness, as evidenced by more than 20% growth in Synopsys continues to represent a compelling opportunity given its leadership in design tools and customizable IP solutions. With chip complexity increasing alongside global demand for AI and AI-enabled systems, the company is positioned to benefit from multiyear customer agreements, deep foundry relationships, and sustained investment in design technology, in our view. As China demand normalizes and delayed revenues are realized, Synopsys is well placed to capture long-term growth across the semiconductor landscape.

Wolverine World Wide (WWW) is a global designer, manufacturer, and marketer of footwear, apparel, and accessories. Its portfolio includes brands like Merrell, Saucony, Sperry, and Wolverine, distributed through wholesale, retail, licensing, and e-commerce channels across more than 170 countries. Shares gained 52.6% in the quarter as the company beat consensus earnings estimates by nearly 45%. Results were supported by strong performances from Saucony, which grew by more than 40% on the back of expanded distribution and strong consumer demand. Gross margins expanded by more than 400 basis points from last year, driven by stronger sales mix, lower promotional activity, and supply chain efficiencies. Investors also responded positively to the company’s relatively low tariff exposure, with less than 10% of production sourced from China.

Wolverine Worldwide continues to represent opportunity as management executes its turnaround plan, with efforts to streamline away from company-owned stores and unprofitable wholesale accounts while continuing to invest in brand development and supply chain capabilities. With strong innovation fueling demand for Saucony and Merrell, spricing power to offset tariffs, and a clear focus on margin expansion, we believe the company is positioned to drive sustained earnings growth and shareholder value.

Another contributor to performance was Sanmina (SANM). Sanmina is a leading electronic manufacturing services provider, offering outsourced design, manufacturing, assembly, and testing solutions across a wide range of industries. Having evolved beyond printed circuit board assembly, Sanmina now delivers more comprehensive, value-added services. Shares gained more than 15% in the quarter as both revenue and earnings exceeded expectations. Revenue was 3% ahead of consensus and earnings nearly 8% higher, driven by margin expansion from a favorable product mix and operational efficiencies. The company also announced the acquisition of ZT Systems’ manufacturing business, a transaction expected to significantly expand capacity and potentially double revenue over the next three years. We believe Sanmina remains well positioned, representing one of the leading providers in the electronic manufacturing services industry, particularly in higher-growth, higher-margin verticals such as medical, automotive, and industrial. Sanmina is well positioned to benefit from its ability to deliver not only component manufacturing but also value-added design and full-system solutions, in our view. This transition toward more comprehensive “sketch-to-scale” engagements enhances profitability and supports long-term growth potential.

Buys and Sells

MDU Resources Group (MDU), which operates in regulated energy delivery, construction materials, and services businesses, serving utility, natural gas, and infrastructure markets across the U.S., was added to the portfolio during the quarter.

IMPORTANT INFORMATION

Risks

Investing involves risk, principal loss is possible. Unlike mutual funds, ETFs may trade at a premium or discount to their net asset value. Harbor ETFs are new and have limited operating history to judge.

There is no guarantee that the investment objective of the Fund will be achieved. Stock markets are volatile and equity values can decline significantly in response to adverse issuer, political, regulatory, market and economic conditions. Stocks of small and mid cap companies pose special risks, including possible illiquidity and greater price volatility than stocks of larger, more established companies. Undervalued securities may take long to appreciate or never reach full value, and value investing may underperform at times compared to other styles.

Benchmarks

The Russell 2500® Value Index is an unmanaged index that is constructed to provide a comprehensive and unbiased barometer of the small to mid-cap value market. This unmanaged index does not reflect fees and expenses and is not available for direct investment. Russell 2500® and Russell® are trademarks of Frank Russell Company.

Disclosures

Expense ratio information is as of the Fund's current prospectus, as supplemented. Gross expenses are the Fund's total annual operating expense.

Due to the security valuation procedures of the Fund and intra-day trading activity not included in the FactSet calculations, the actual returns may vary. From time to time the cash return in the portfolio may appear distorted based on the way FactSet’s attribution calculation methodology addresses delayed settlements.

Best and Worst Performers sections reflect stocks in the portfolio for the quarter with an average weight of 0.25% or greater.

Views expressed herein are drawn from commentary provided to Harbor by the subadvisor and may not be reflective of their current opinions or future actions, are subject to change without prior notice, and should not be considered investment advice.

This information should not be considered as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. The weightings, holdings, industries, sectors, countries, and returns mentioned may change at any time and may not represent current or future investments.

As a result of changing market conditions, total net asset levels, expenses and other statistics may change at any time and may differ from those shown.

The total amount shown for sector, industries, or country holdings may be greater than 100% because of the inclusion of derivatives and the collateral securities supporting those instruments.

Sector allocations are determined using the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS), which is a service of Morgan Stanley Capital International (MSCI) and Standard & Poor's (S&P).

Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a fund before investing. To obtain a summary prospectus or prospectus for this and other information, visit Active ETFs or call 800-422-1050. Read it carefully before investing.

Foreside Fund Services, LLC is the Distributor of the Harbor ETFs.

Attribution Disclosures

Linked Performance by Sectors data is produced from FactSet using data supplied by State Street Bank and Trust Company. Active Currency Contribution is the Currency Contribution of the portfolio minus the Currency Contribution of the benchmark.

Allocation Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return that is attributable to taking different group bets from the benchmark. (If either the portfolio or the benchmark has no position in a given group, allocation effect is the lone effect.) A group's allocation effect equals the average percent capitalization of the portfolio's group minus the average percent cap of the benchmark's group times the total return of the benchmark group minus the total return of the benchmark.

Average Weight is the dollar value (price times the shares held) of the security or group, divided by the total dollar value of the entire portfolio displayed as a percentage. It is calculated as the simple arithmetic average of daily values.

Contribution to Return is the contribution of a security or group to the overall portfolio return. It is calculated as the security weight multiplied by the daily security return linked daily across the reporting period.

Currency Contribution is Total Return in USD subtracting out the Local Returns.

Local Returns are the Total Return of the portfolio or benchmark using the local currency.

Selection Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return attributable to choosing different securities within groups from the benchmark. A group's security selection effect equals the average weight of the benchmark's group times the total return of the portfolio's group minus the total return of the benchmark's group.

Total Effect is the sum of Allocation Effect and Selection Effect. The total effect represents the opportunity cost of what was done in a group relative to the overall portfolio. It is not just the difference between percent contribution in the portfolio and benchmark. At the overall portfolio level, the two numbers are equal. At the group level, they can be different.

Total Return is the price change of a security or group including dividends accrued over the report period (or the in-portfolio return) which includes only the time period that each security was in the portfolio.

Definitions

Median Market Cap: The median size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares.

Weighted Average Market Capitalization: The average size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares. Earnings per share (EPS) is the portion of a company's profit allocated to each outstanding share.

The Est 3-5 Yr EPS Growth (%) is the estimated growth of earnings per share over the next 3-5 years, using pre-calculated mean long-term EPS growth rate estimates, which are calculated using each individual broker’s methodology, from FactSet, First Call, I/B/E/S Consensus, and Reuters. Forward looking estimates may not come to pass.

% EPS Growth - Past 3 Year: Earnings per share refers to the bottom-line measure of a company’s profitability defined as net income divided by the number of outstanding shares. The Adjusted Trailing P/E (price-to-earnings) Ratio is the closing stock price divided by the sum of the last 12 months actual EPS.

Forecasted P/E Ratio: a measure of the P/E (price-to-earnings) ratio using forecasted earnings for the P/E calculation. Return on Equity (ROE) is a measure of financial performance calculated by dividing net income by shareholders' equity. The Price/Book (price-to-book) Ratio evaluates a firm's market value relative to its book value.

All P/E, ROE and P/B statistics are calculated as weighted medians.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Reynolds Consumer Products: It's A Wrap (Downgrade)
data.description:
Back in July of this year, I decided to revisit Reynolds Consumer Products (REYN) after my previous article about it had been written two years earlier. During that time, the company woefully underperformed the market. But I argued, in my article this year, that shares looked cheap, even with recent weak spots factored in. At the end of the day, I ended up concluding that it made for a good "Buy" candidate. And since then, things have gone quite well. Shares are up 15.2%, while the S&P 500 is up 10.5%. But unfortunately, all good things must come to an end. While shares of the company certainly aren't expensive, they aren't cheap compared to other similar firms right now. And even though revenue continues to grow, bottom-line results have continued worsening. Given this mix of factors, I believe that downgrading it to a "Hold" is the right choice at this moment.

It’s a wrap for Reynolds Consumer Products

If you live in the United States, you almost certainly know about Reynolds Consumer Products. After all, at least one of its products are in 95% of all households across the country. And over half of all the revenue the company generates comes from products that hold the No. 1 position in their categories. These offerings include certain cooking and baking products like aluminum foil, disposable aluminum pans, parchment paper, freezer paper, wax paper, butcher paper, Hefty branded waste and storage products, Hefty tableware products like plates and containers, and Presto products that include things like food storage bags, trash bags, and plastic wrap.

Lately, the financial picture for the company has been mixed. For the most recent quarter, which is the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, revenue amounted to $931 million. That's an increase of 2.3% over the $910 million the company reported a year earlier. Even though revenue for the company improved, profitability worsened. Net income dropped from $86 million to $79 million. But the good news is that, on an adjusted basis, it actually increased slightly from $86 million to $88 million. Sadly, most of the other profitability metrics have worsened. Operating cash flow dropped from $124 million to $93 million. However, if we adjust for changes in working capital, we do get a rise from $114 million to $141 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company dipped from $171 million to $168 million.

These mixed results have become something of a trend. In the chart above, you can see results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. Revenue increased, but each one of its profitability metrics worsened. If I can choose between declining revenue with increasing profits and cash flows, or rising revenue with declining profits and cash flows, I will pick the former any day of the week. So this on its own is discouraging. But to really understand what's going on, we need to dig a bit deeper.

At its core, Reynolds Consumer Products has four different operating segments. All of these are surprisingly similar in size, which is not something that I am accustomed to. With most companies, you tend to have significant disparities, with one segment leading the charge by a large margin. But that's not the case here. Even so, the largest of these segments is its Reynolds Cooking & Baking segment. And the most recent data provided by management shows that it is actually doing exceptionally well. In the chart above, you can see revenue and EBITDA for it for the most recent quarter compared to the same time last year, as well as for the first nine months of this year compared to the same time last year.

According to management, the Reynolds Cooking & Baking segment has benefited from higher pricing that more than offset lower volumes. In the company's quarterly report, it didn't really provide much in the way of detail beyond this. But in their third-quarter earnings call, the CEO of the company boiled this outperformance down to two things. The first of these is innovation. And the second is that this is a "performance brand." I interpret this as management basically saying that this is an industry leader that is recognized for its quality, and something that consumers will buy even when times are tough. This might seem at odds when you consider the decline in volume that the segment saw. But management specified that the drop in volume was actually better than the firm saw in other segments, and that outperformance was due primarily to tailwinds from incremental cooking at home. Food at home dining is more likely to experience demand for these types of offerings. But because of budget issues, more consumers are trying to cut costs. Although it is entirely possible that volume could still decline, I would say that as economic conditions worsen, at least relative to other operating segments, this part of the company will probably outperform.

Growth in revenue was also experienced when it came to the Hefty Waste & Storage segment. Revenue expanded from $253 million to $266 million. And with that, profitability inched up from $72 million to $74 million. Higher volumes actually were the primary driver here. This can be chalked up to promotional activity and name brand recognition based on my own interpretation of management’s comments. But higher promotional activity does translate to lower margins. Overall profit margins here contracted from 28.46% to 27.82%. But in the grand scheme of things, this is only a mild inconvenience.

The last segment that saw revenue growth during the quarter was Presto Products. Its sales grew because of higher volumes. The result here was a rise in revenue from $150 million to $163 million. And profitability inched up from $33 million to $35 million. And then lastly, we have the Hefty Tableware segment, which was a true drag on the company. Lower foam volume ultimately pushed revenue down sharply from $222 million to $200 million. But the good news is that management was able to push higher costs onto customers, more than offsetting that pain on the bottom line. And as a result, profits grew from $26 million to $29 million.

On the whole, things certainly look mixed. Having said that, for the year as a whole, it looks like 2025 will end up being worse than 2024. Even though revenue is currently up so far this year, management actually expects revenue to be flat at best and down 1% at worst. They also expect adjusted net profits at the midpoint to be around $341 million. This would compare negatively to the $352 million the company reported for last year. Meanwhile, EBITDA is on track to decline from $678 million last year to between $655 million and $665 million this year. In all likelihood, hitting the midpoint here would mean adjusted operating cash flow of about $464 million.

With these estimates, and also with the historical results that the company achieved for 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. These multiples certainly aren't bad. But they aren't great either. And relative to other similar firms, the company looks to be a tad pricey. In the table below, I stacked it up against five similar enterprises. What I found was that, on a price-to-earnings basis, four of the five companies I compared it to ended up being cheaper than it is. And when it came to the other two trading multiple approaches, three of the five ended up being cheaper.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Reynolds Consumer Products 14.7 10.8 10.0
WD-40 Company (WDFC) 28.9 29.8 23.0
Central Garden & Pet Company (CENT) 13.3 6.5 7.8
Energizer Holdings (ENR) 5.8 9.5 6.8
Spectrum Brands Holdings (SPB) 15.9 7.8 7.5
The Clorox Company (CLX) 16.1 14.9 12.5

Takeaway

The way I see it, Reynolds Consumer Products remains an interesting prospect. But I don't find it to be the most compelling opportunity right now. The stock has risen nicely since I reaffirmed my bullish rating on it. And on top of this, bottom-line results are worsening. When you add on top of this how the stock is priced on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms, I believe downgrading it to a "Hold" is the right choice.

data.title: Q3 2025 GDP: Consumer Resilience Anchors Hot Q3 Growth
data.description:
Third quarter economic growth smashed expectations and blew by the Atlanta Fed’s forecast this morning. Real GDP expanded at a seasonally adjusted annual rate (SAAR) of 4.3% in Q3 2025, the strongest growth since Q3 2023 and above expectations of a print around 3.3% (analysts’ consensus) to 3.5% (Atlanta Fed nowcast). Growth was led by consumer spending, while investment was a very minor drag, and the contribution from trade was positive. Let’s dive into the details.

GDP Segments

The consumer was the star in Q3 2025 as personal consumption expenditures increased at a SAAR of 3.5%, the strongest so far this year and only slightly trailing behind PCE growth in the last three quarters of 2024. Spending was strong across both goods and services which saw growth rates of 3.1% and 3.7%, respectively. Notably, the pace of services spending was the strongest since Q3 2022. The contribution of consumer spending to overall GDP growth was 2.39 ppts, the largest contributor of the main GDP segments. The Q3 result speaks to the resiliency of the consumer who has faced many headwinds this year.

While consumer spending was robust, the private domestic investment data left a lot to be desired. The segment was basically unchanged in Q3, declining at a SAAR of -0.3%, which is the second straight quarterly contraction. The real estate sector was a drag on growth for the third straight quarter as residential investment fell -5.1%, offsetting nonresidential investment growth of 2.8%. Within non-residential investment, equipment (+5.4%) and intellectual property (+5.4%) saw solid growth, dampened by a weakening in investment in structures (-6.3%) for the seventh straight quarter. Technology investment led once again in Q3, with information processing (+$12.5 billion) and R&D (+$14.0 billion) leading in the raw investment numbers.

The interplay of inventory and trade were sources of volatility in Q1 and Q2 growth data, but the dynamics in Q3 were not as drastic. Net trade contributed 1.59 ppts to GDP growth as exports expanded at a SAAR of 8.8% and imports fell at a SAAR of -4.7%. This was slightly offset by a -$11.4 billion drop in private inventories, which contributed -0.22 ppts to growth. Excluding the positive contribution by trade, we see final sales to domestic purchasers growth at 2.9%, slower than the headline real GDP growth but still the strongest so far this year.

Price Indexes

GDP data also comes with some price indexes, though these updates will be basically obsolete in the context of the current inflation outlook. The gross domestic purchases price index increased at a 3.4% annualized rate and was up 2.7% YoY in Q3 2025, accelerating from 2.3% YoY in Q2 to the highest level since Q3 2023. The Fed’s preferred inflation gauge, the core PCE price index, grew at a slower annualized pace of 2.9% and saw an acceleration from 2.7% YoY in Q2 to 2.9% YoY in Q3. These data points suggest that pricing pressures saw a significant increase in Q3 2025, which may or may not be traced back to tariffs. Indeed, the strong consumer spending impulse in the quarter could have been the source of upward pressure from the demand side.

Corporate Profits

Since this first GDP report was not the advance estimate and more consistent with the traditional first revision, it included corporate profit data for Q3 2025. The data showed a strong $166.1 billion increase in overall corporate profits, well above the $6.8 billion gain in Q2 and the -$47.8 billion contraction in Q1. Domestic profits (after inventory adjustments) increased by $154.3 billion, with financial profits up $54.0 billion and nonfinancial profits up $100.4 billion. Overall, it is clear that corporations enjoyed a solid quarter of profits. After tax, inventory-adjusted profits increased 7.0% QoQ and were up 4.2% YoY.

Market Reaction

The GDP report was much hotter than expected as it showed growth well above forecasts. While the data are old, they showed that the resiliency of the consumer can continue to push the U.S. economy forward, even when private investment is sluggish. Interestingly, the market responded as if this result was not priced in, as there were some notes of hawkishness.

  • Equity futures trended slightly negative in the hour after the Q3 GDP report was released, but perked up after the New York session began. The S&P 500 (SP500) eventually moved higher about two hours after the GDP release, up around 0.2%, but trading had hawkish notes since the NASDAQ lagged, showing no growth, and the interest rate-sensitive small caps, Russell 2000 (RTY), were down -0.5%.
  • The bond market moved lower on the stronger growth data, pushing Treasury rates up a few basis points. The movement was strongest in the middle of the yield curve, with the 2-year and 5-year Treasury yields both up by about 3 bps. The 10-year yield was only up about 2 bps, and the 30-year yield hadn’t moved much at all. Overall, it looks like the strong GDP report shifted the bond market’s sentiment on short-term prospects for growth while longer-term prospects were mostly unchanged.
  • The US dollar index (DXY) has struggled in the last month, down -2.0%, but today’s strong growth data helped it recoup some of those losses. The index improved from being down at most -0.4% this morning to a loss of about -0.1% about two hours after the GDP report was released.

The Q3 GDP report arrives at an awkward but still useful moment in the information landscape. On the surface, the data are clearly lagging, with the quarter now several months behind us and Q4 data only weeks away. However, the data still matter because the upcoming Q4 reading is likely to be heavily distorted by the government shutdown, complicating the interpretation of near-term growth and activity. In that sense, Q3 may end up serving as the most recent clean snapshot of how the U.S. economy was functioning under relatively normal conditions. On the other hand, the price indexes in the Q3 report are undeniably hot and point to renewed inflation pressure during the quarter, but their informational value is limited given that CPI data already extend through November and provide a more current read on pricing dynamics.

The broader takeaway from the Q3 GDP data is the continued resilience of the consumer despite persistent price pressures and a gradually cooling labor market. Strong consumption growth aligns with the prevailing view that holiday spending is holding up, with year-over-year trends tracking in the mid to high single digits. At the same time, the report pushes back against the idea that the U.S. economy is being driven almost exclusively by AI-related activity. That characterization fits better within pockets of nonresidential investment, particularly technology and intellectual property, but it does not describe the broader growth picture, which remains anchored in household spending rather than a narrow investment boom.

And if the consumer really is this resilient, price pressures would be seen as being stronger going into the new year. As such, the Fed would have to respect that inflationary impulse and back away from further easing. The market seems to be reacting to a situation like this, pushing the January cut probability even lower to just 13.3%, down from 19.9% yesterday and 24.4% a week ago. There is still a lot of data to come out before the January 27-28th meeting, but after today’s hot Q3 GDP report, the bias towards weaker macro data has been diminished.

data.title: VYM Vs. XYLD: Why The Dividend Growth ETF Beats The Popular High Yield Play
data.description:
Recently, covered call ETFs have become much more popular among income investors, especially those whose underlying indexes are either the S&P 500 (SPY) or the NASDAQ (QQQ). The reason for this is that they seem to give investors the best of all worlds, which includes strong underlying performance stemming from the high-performing mega-cap-tech-dominated indices, along with attractive monthly distributions that come from the covered call writing strategies employed by these funds.

A big source of justification for investors who choose these funds comes from both the higher yield and the higher total returns that they’ve delivered in recent years relative to many popular dividend growth ETFs. However, I think this analysis is short-sighted, and in today’s article, I am going to argue why one popular dividend growth ETF, the Vanguard High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF (VYM), makes for a better long-term dividend investment than a popular covered call ETF, the Global X S&P 500® Covered Call ETF (XYLD), does.

The Structural Difference Between VYM And XYLD

In particular, while XYLD is well diversified, given that it holds the S&P 500 as its underlying index, making it a potential one-stop shop for a passive income investor, I think that VYM is an even better diversified and overall superior option to XYLD. Additionally, you could always couple it with a couple of other higher-yielding diversifying funds like the Reeves Utility Income CEF (UTG), the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP), the Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI), and the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ), to boost the portfolio's aggregate yield while also further enhancing the portfolio's diversification, which would create a better overall risk-adjusted structure and more sustainable, attractive yield than XYLD does.

To better understand why VYM is a better core holding for an income investor than XYLD, we need to first look at how they are laid out. VYM tracks the FTSE High Dividend Yield Index, which screens for companies with above-average dividends. This translates into a portfolio with well over 500 holdings and oftentimes nearly 600 holdings (571 at present). In addition, it has broad sector exposure, with its largest exposure being to financials, technology, and healthcare, while industrials, consumer defensive stocks, and energy all have meaningful exposure in the portfolio as well.

It also manages to place pretty big bets on its top holdings, with its top 10 making up about 28% of its total portfolio. Particularly notable is the fact that VYM has a major AI player and a strong dividend growth stock in Broadcom (AVGO) as its top holding, making up a whopping 8.69% of its total portfolio.

Meanwhile, XYLD owns the underlying S&P 500 index, which means it actually has fewer holdings than VYM does, and it is also much more heavily exposed to mega-cap tech, which makes up over a third (~35%) of its total portfolio, then writes at-the-money index calls on the S&P 500 to generate very attractive monthly distributions, which have amounted to about a 12% trailing 12-month yield. It also makes its distributions monthly, which is an attractive feature for retirees. However, the at-the-money call writing strategy means that its upside is significantly capped, and this is seen from the fund’s long-term price performance, which has effectively been flat over the long term, even as the S&P 500 has rallied significantly.

Additionally, XYLD has a 10 times higher expense ratio than VYM does at 0.6% compared to 0.06% for VYM. Not only that, but XYLD has generated virtually nothing in terms of dividend growth over the long term, whereas VYM has grown its dividend for 19 straight years, and over the past 10 years, it has grown it at a 5% CAGR.

While the appeal of XYLD is clear, given its high current yield and exposure to the S&P 500, there are better ways to achieve these outcomes, including with a portfolio that has VYM at its core. This is because over the long term, VYM has outperformed XYLD on a total return basis over time. In fact, VYM's stock price performance alone (i.e., not counting its dividends) has outpaced XYLD's total returns over time, while XYLD's options writing strategy has failed to do much to smooth out its sequence of returns risk since those are very closely correlated with VYM's stock price performance, as evidenced in the chart below:

A Better Way To Build Sustainable Income

However, for investors who need a higher income yield than VYM offers in order to mitigate sequence of returns risk in retirement and live off of dividends alone, you can pair VYM with other higher-yielding funds. For example, combining UTG, AMLP, and RQI with VYM can generate an aggregate portfolio yield in the mid-single digits, while still generating strong dividend growth over time.

While VYM also still gives some decent tech exposure, for investors who want more mega-cap tech exposure, they can always add a Nasdaq-focused covered call ETF like GPIQ to the portfolio to bring in a high-yielding source of mega-cap tech exposure that’s more concentrated than what XYLD offers, since these funds are based on the NASDAQ 100 (NDX) underlying index instead of the S&P 500.

Investor Takeaway

While covered call funds are understandably popular right now, given their strong relative performance against dividend growth funds and their very attractive yields, there are much better ways to get exposure to that space than via XYLD. Additionally, for investors who want durable and sustainably growing dividend income along with capital appreciation, I would prefer to steer clear of covered call ETFs altogether, or at least keep them a very small part of the portfolio, and instead invest in quality dividend growth funds like VYM, and perhaps supplement them with either some high-yield funds like those mentioned in this article, or even better yet, build a portfolio of high-quality, undervalued, high-yielding stocks from the REITs (VNQ), MLPs, BDCs (BIZD), and broader infrastructure sectors.

This is what we do, and we have achieved considerable long-term total return outperformance in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: GDXY: Not Enough Upside, Too Aggressive On Income
data.description:
Back in April, when I was near the peak of "pounding the table" about gold and gold miners (even from before that, but I really started going around that time), I covered the YieldMax Gold Miners Option Income Strategy ETF (GDXY). I gave it a Hold rating, not because it was a bad time to consider gold or gold miners, but because I felt like owning the income variant of GDX, the global gold miners index that GDXY is based on, was a better bet in the short-term. I saw that it had the potential for explosive upside, and I wouldn't want to limit that.

Maybe I was a little harsh in saying that YieldMax was "striking out" on gold, but the expectations I had played out. Even assuming all dividends reinvested (which is the best way I can show returns, but is unreasonable for income investors), GDXY trailed GDX by a significant margin. This chart starts at the publication date of my first article on GDXY, April 22nd.

But Income is Income

I understand that income investors are often not phased by the chart above, and some even get angry with me (vocal minority, I assume) when I bring it up, see the comments sections of most YieldMax ETF articles.

On the first part, I understand the idea: if you need income, and you're interested in earning it without selling shares, trading your upside for immediate, high income could be a reasonable trade-off. When it comes to most stocks, or even gold itself, I get it.

My problem with GDXY isn't about income vehicles. I understand that trading upside for high immediate income is the deal that income investors want when they buy these funds. They're just willing to accept that top chart and so going on and on about it doesn't make a lot of sense -- they don't care, that's already assumed going into the trade.

My problem with GDXY is similar across almost all YieldMax funds, and that is that I believe they are too aggressive with their income generation. They get so aggressive that their funds tend to kick out insanely high distribution rates, which can be alluring for investors, but it leads to dramatic underperformance in the long term because reinvestment is necessary to stabilize the funds long-term.

GDX's distribution rate is currently sitting at 58.2%. And it leads to a chart like this, showing the difference in return of price and total return. If we assume that income investors keep all the income that is spit out from the fund instead of reinvesting, investors who bought at GDXY's inception are now down nearly 15%. That means that moving forward, assuming distribution rates remain the same, their investment will earn a lower nominal dollar amount next year.

And this will continue, compounding downward. Keep in mind that this negative return happened in one of the most explosive bull markets for the underlying asset in the last decade. In a gold miner bear market, I would expect GDXY to drop even further, and without reinvestment from income investors, their nominal income stream will continue to shrink over time in.

It may stay by percentage terms, but income investors typically don't spend money in percentage terms, they spend money in nominal terms.

We can see this play out in the nominal dividends paid out by the fund. Keep in mind that YieldMax switched from monthly to weekly paying, and so the drop in amount earlier this year is due to the distribution frequency changes. The "D" symbol below looks cluttered, but I needed to include it to show when the dividends were actually paid and to show why the large drop in dividends paid. If we average the weeklies back out to a month, then the previous trend is clearer.

If we total up November's dividend amounts, it comes out to $0.58, on the low end of the trend, but not hurting so badly yet. But again, this is a problem that will come up after owning this for years, not something that will come up after a couple of months.

GDXY Overview

Now that I've said my piece on YieldMax (yes, I understand the irony of saying that a company with that name is too aggressive), let's dig into the fund itself.

GDXY fundamentally is a bet on the global gold miners index (which the VanEck GDX ETF captures), with the upside capped in favor of immediate, high income. This is the trade that covered calls inherently come with, and one that income investors expect when employing this fund.

GDXY itself was brought to market in May 2024, carries a gross expense ratio of 1.08%, has net assets in excess of $222M, and pays its dividends weekly.

Like most of YieldMax's strategies, this one also relies on synthetic options, meaning that call and put options are married together to create the same exposure profile as holding long stock, but without the transaction costs of holding long stock. This allows them to hold nearly the entire portfolio in treasuries while still gaining the exposure to gold. In the holdings, we can even see longer-dated holdings, such as an obligation due in 2031.

Once a synthetic position is created, credit spreads, which are more capital efficient for YieldMax to employ, are sold short to limit the upside and generate the high income.

Looking at the options held by GDXY, I was able to produce this chart, which shows the current profit and loss profile of GDXY. Note that after publication of this article, this will likely be incorrect, as YieldMax is able to trade intraday and often changes their holdings day-to-day to accommodate for the fast-moving nature of the options market. I would expect the overall structure of the chart to remain the same.

This cap on the upside, although not entirely flat and slightly elevated, does create a situation where investors are not meeting their full upside potential and instead prioritizing income. That priority creates a 58% distribution rate, but it means that investors will be lagging behind dramatically. More than I believe most investors would like to lag behind.

Of course, the trade-off is up to the individual. If you're an income investor who does need 58% distribution rates, which GDXY has provided so far, and that's notable.

The Gold Miner Thesis

To be bullish on GDXY, one needs to necessarily be bullish on its underlying asset, GDX, the global gold miner's index. These firms can be very volatile (a primary reason they were selected by YieldMax to be a covered call target), much more than gold itself, and that is what many investors want when investing in them. There is extra upside (and downside) for gold miners over gold itself as an asset. Here is the YTD of both assets for reference:

There are good reasons to be bullish on gold itself right now, which would translate to gold miner bullishness. From a recent article I wrote earlier this month, "Gold Has a New Buyer, A Different Kind of Central Bank," I discussed how institutional buying is still making up much of the gold demand, and that new kinds of institutions were buying gold, like stablecoin issuer Tether.

I see the structural demand for gold as growing, and this includes not only central banks but now also another kind of central bank: cryptocurrency issuers. Gold is being bought up as part of a means of backing USDT, which makes up 60% of the stablecoin market. This is a huge shift, and one that shouldn't be ignored.

Of course, speculation is still rampant, and gold's recent volatility has proven that while U.S. investors are not accumulating and allocating to gold like those in the rest of the world, they are still trading it heavily. I expect that volatility to continue moving forward, but that is not necessarily a bad thing. It does warrant caution.

This translates to demand for gold miners who provide the supply to meet it, and who disproportionately benefit during run-ups in gold itself, as they can lock in high prices with futures contracts and make significant returns. The index itself is made up of giants in the space, such as Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM) and Newmont Corp (NEM), who have excelled in the space during this latest leg of the gold bull market.

Of course, if gold were to turn, gold miners would likely turn with it and likely be more volatile, so this is a risk to consider.

A structural turn would require central banks to slow or halt their purchases significantly, breaking current trends across the globe. This may happen suddenly, especially if central banks that are working to de-dollarize their holdings are unwilling to finance more gold purchases at current levels. I do not seriously consider this a threat right now, because current demand seems to be fairly linear, but if central banks do halt their buying, it will likely come all at once (just like their demand seemed to do).

Much of gold's momentum is based on these institutional trends continuing to play out, and there is an "ETF trapdoor" so to speak because many of the publicly available tools investors can use to speculate on the price of gold being are backed by paper gold and not physical gold. This means that a sell off in these assets, also sparked by speculation, would not cause much physical gold to actually change hands, but could affect the price dramatically.

That would affect the valuations of gold miners, as they would be able to fetch lower prices for the gold they produce, and I would expect GDX and subsequently GDXY to have an even worse response.

An Alternative Fund With More Upside

I am not a huge fan of Roundhill ETFs, generally speaking, for many of the same reasons as I'm not a huge fan of YieldMax ETFs. I believe that they tend to be overly aggressive with their options positioning, which creates situations with unsustainable levels of distribution. Over many years, the erosion of NAV without reinvestment and the erosion of principal without reinvestment will be problematic for future income streams. While the percentages may remain the same, the nominal income would be expected to drop over time as you have less and less principal to generate income with.

But when I pit GDXY against Roundhill's alternative, GDXW, which has a far shorter lifespan and so the comparison is limited, I see a fund that has significantly more upside to it. Here we can see both funds capturing the explosive growth of gold miners that happened in this last month, with GDXW coming out 10% ahead in total return.

From a price perspective, it's worse. But it also highlights how low the payments are for GDXW than they are for GDXY. An investor who is only focused on the income may actually prefer the lower performance for the higher income. I believe that that's folly in the long term, but I understand where the impulse would come from and why it may appear optimal now if current income is more valuable than future income to you.

GDXY and GDXW are comparable because they have incredibly similar strategies, both focused on selling options against GDX for income. GDXW has a lower expense ratio at 0.99%, although I believe the difference between the two is splitting hairs at the end of the day as they are so close, but fee-sensitive investors should take note that GDXY's expense ratio is 1.08%.

The major difference between the two, and what explains the differences in return, is that GDXW uses swaps to generate their returns, and seeks for 1.2x leverage against GDX while it caps the upside, meaning that it should have more upside capture in general, but does come with some extra risk.

To this end, GDXW has one holding:

In a bullish market, this translates to excess gains, as we can see. However, it could also work against the fund in a bearish scenario. Since I am bullish on gold miners, this seems like a good trade-off to me, but make sure that your risk tolerance would allow for this if you decide to look into GDXW as an alternative to GDXY.

A Note on Taxes

One thing YieldMax does have going for it, although this also applies to Roundhill and all other ETFs that use options to generate income, is that they are very tax-efficient.

When distributed through an ETF structure, options gains and losses are treated as return of capital ("ROC"). This is considered advantageous because ROC does not generate taxable events. Instead, it counts against your cost basis, and so taxes are deferred so long as you continue to hold the shares. If you hold a fund long enough, your cost basis can go to zero. Any distributions after that will create taxable events but be counted as long-term capital gains in the year that you receive the distributions.

This makes GDXY not only more efficient than trading the options yourself, but also makes it viable for taxable accounts because it could reduce tax liabilities given that your long-term capital gains rate is lower than your income tax rate, which is usually true for most people.

In the latest 19A-1 form released by YieldMax ETFs, GDXY's distribution was estimated at 95% return of capital. Last year's final ROC amount, found on this IRS form published on the YieldMax ETFs website, was at 68%.

Conclusion

Ultimately, I am keeping my Hold rating on GDXY, not because I am less bullish on gold miners at all. In fact, you can read my recent buy for a gold miner-linked ETF, GDMN, here.

No, my Hold rating stays because I do not believe that the YieldMax strategy is the best option for income investors to consider. It is not a bad option, and for that reason, I'm not giving it a Sell rating. But it is definitely not the most optimal strategy given that I believe it over-distributes and is overly aggressive in its option trading, which will lead to a necessarily crumbling net asset value over time. From my view, that strategy will not lead to long-term success in the fund without necessary reinvestment.

Because I don't like to advise income investors to reinvest portions of their income, although that would make the strategy more attractive (and could make GDXY a Buy rating given my bullish outlook on gold miners), I must keep my Hold rating on GDXY. For now, investors should consider other options like uncapped exposure to gold and gold miners, as well as GDXW, which seems to distribute less and capture more upside in the index -- something I find preferable in our current environment.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: V.F. Corp.: Continued Drag From Lackluster Brand Power
data.description:
Summary

V.F. Corporation (VFC) is a retail company with a history of mergers and acquisitions. In an effort to improve key brands like Timberland, The North Face, and Vans, management is currently executing a “reinvent” turnaround to cut costs, grow revenues, and lower its debt. But the prevailing retail picture is not great, with tariffs and lackluster brand power creating headwinds for the business over the next six to twelve months. Because the company has relied on acquisitions, a key question remains as to whether or not management can execute a turnaround organically. With previous goodwill impairments as an indicator, V.F. Corporation may find it difficult to grab share of consumer preferences, making for a decent short opportunity in a difficult macroenvironment.

Background: Annual Report, Second Quarter Summary

V.F. Corporation is a retail trading company that owns multiple brands, including Vans, The North Face, and Timberland, among others. Management recently sold off the Dickies brand, leaving VFC with ten total brands. The company has a long history of mergers & divestitures; notably, they acquired Vans in 2004 for $396 million, The North Face for a mere $25 million in 2000, and Timberland for $2 billion in 2011. But the history of M&A has led to lackluster stock performance, with VFC share prices dropping over 75% in the past five years. Now, at a time when the company is facing revenue declines and brand impairments, management must fix the balance sheet by reducing debt, streamlining cost cutting, and “reinventing” itself in order to get back on track. As of the most recent Annual Report, CEO Bracken Darrell highlighted reduced net debt by 26%, cost savings of $300 million, and a Vans brand turnaround, and as of Q2 fiscal 2026, the sale of Dickies for $600 million in order to pay down debt.

While each of the aforementioned initiatives has been positive for V.F. Corp., the question remains as to whether or not management can brand-rebuild (specifically Vans) when most of its history has been M&A focused. In the meantime, through fiscal Q2, revenues have been roughly flat year over year, interest expenses have crept up from $90 to $93 million, and inventory has drifted upward over the past three months. If I were to be reflective on the second quarter (three months ending September 30th), I would say that both COGS and SG&A expenses were down, leading to a solid $75 million increase in operating income. Furthermore, the inventory build may just be seasonality as management prepares for the holiday season. On the second-quarter conference call, the CEO highlighted 4% revenue growth for The North Face, 4% growth for Timberland, and great results for Altra (forecasting $250 million in yearly revenue). But with Vans revenue down 11%, the brand will continue to be a drag on earnings, and I would not be surprised to see impairments down the road. On top of that, VFC is navigating a difficult macroeconomic environment for retail, as it relates to tariffs, a Q3 forecast for revenues down 1-3%, and the waning strength of the consumer.

Tariffs and the Consumer

Even though V.F. Corp. spreads out its sourcing, with 273 independent contractors in 30 countries, they are still exposed to tariff risk at the hands of the current White House Administration. Per the Annual Report, 85% of the products are sourced from Southeast Asia, Central, and South America (p. 26). The report notes Vietnam, Bangladesh, Cambodia, and Indonesia are the top four countries for sourcing. As an aside, I will lean briefly on Nike’s (NKE) Q2 results, as the company’s margins and inventory were affected by tariffs; notably, Nike’s gross margin decreased three percent, and inventory costs were higher. Specific to the Nike conference call, the CEO stated that tariffs are presenting a headwind to any margin expansion. Anecdotally, the company manufactures 51%, 28%, and 17% of its footwear in Vietnam, Indonesia, and China (respectively), as well as 31%, 15%, and 15% of its apparel in Vietnam, China, and Cambodia (respectively; see Nike’s Annual Report, p. 3). The recent performance of Nike as a retail bellwether points toward additional tariff risk for V.F. Corp.; the “reinvent” strategy employed by management may not be enough to stave off industry headwinds. Even though the CEO is projecting $500-$600 million in operating income expansion, that target may come well beyond 2028.

The overarching state of the consumer is one of higher housing costs and lower discretionary spending. According to the National Retail Federation, November 2025 core retail sales showed 4.66% growth year over year but were flat month over month; specifically, clothing and accessories ticked down 0.04% month over month. It is perhaps the calm before the holiday swell, but recent data by the NRF regarding holiday trends indicate the consumer is holding back a bit on holiday spending.

In the slide above, we can see that consumer spending is expected to drop about 2% ($13 per person) from $641 to $628, with roughly half of that expected to go toward clothing and accessories:

Given the above data, there is an important dichotomy between 1) V.F. Corp. management projecting revenues down 1-3% in their most important quarter of the year and 2) Consumer retail growing 4.66% year over year (while holiday spending will tick down) that speaks to a long-term issue with V.F. Corp.’s branding and its ability to turn around the business organically. In my opinion, over the next twelve months tariff issues will persist, and the consumer will remain pressured by inflation & costs, making VFC shares a sell.

Goodwill Impairments and Valuation

We’ve mentioned previously that V.F. Corp. has been an amalgamation of acquisitions since its founding. That said, it is worth looking at some of these deals in order to gauge valuation metrics. The company acquired Dickies in 2017 for $820 million, and Vans was acquired in 2004—both for approximately 1x sales. The $2 billion Timberland acquisition in 2011 added about $700 million in sales at the time, making the EV/sales multiple roughly 2.8x. The one standout has been The North Face, which was near bankruptcy until VF management scooped up the business for a total enterprise value of $155 million; that year, The North Face reportedly generated $238 million in sales (a 0.65x EV/sales multiple). If we read the tea leaves, anything between 0.65x and, say, 2.8x EV/Sales is a decent multiple, assuming a good macroeconomic climate.

Per Seeking Alpha, at $18.50/share, VFC trades around 1.3x forward EV/Sales. Perhaps it is trading at fair value; however, the business is operating in a difficult retail environment with flailing brand power (re: Vans and the “reinvent” turnaround). As such, in my opinion a 1x multiple is appropriate, leading toward a price of $15/share or lower. With Vans revenues down 16% annually, and downtrodden revenue guidance for the holiday quarter, I believe the downside is much more. Note that with the Dickies sale alone, V.F. Corp. lost roughly $120 million on the deal.

Something to watch out for in the coming quarters includes goodwill impairments, if for no other reason than that is what has happened in the past. In 2024, management identified $507.6 million in impairments for the Dickies, Icebreaker, and Timberland brands. One year later, there were additional impairments of $89.2 million for Dickies (p. 28 of the Annual Report). While there have been no impairment charges for the first two quarters of fiscal 2026, note that the past impairments have occurred in the third & fourth fiscal quarters—right where we are at now. Additionally, I will be keeping a close eye on balance sheet inventory over the next six months. If holiday sales are less than expected, inventories could remain high, forcing management into higher discounting next year.

Risks

While I am anticipating a rough six months for the business, I could be wrong on several fronts. First off, we know that retail spending has increased year over year across the board: if VFC manages to capture more of that spend, then the company could execute its turnaround and increase gross profit by the expected $500-$600 million goal set by management. The crux of my thesis largely depends upon the state of the consumer as well as the Vans brand's drag on sales. On the latest conference call, the CEO mentioned product initiatives for Vans. Consider his commentary below in response to a Vans question on revenue growth:

“So our expectation is as we keep rolling in newer and newer product into the stores, we’re going to see more and more performance, and yes, we’re obviously upgrading our marketing. […] You’ll see a shift away from the skate-only marketing into really that plus a lot more. You’ll see surfers, you’ll see a lot more product. […] So we’re just going to keep pouring it on. This is a fundamentals business. You got to be in the right places with the right products with the right story. And we think we’re really going to have that as we go forward.”

If the Vans brand ends up catching on with consumers, it would be a great success for management (and bad for a short position). Time will tell, but for now I lean on the side of declining brand power.

Similarly, the tariff risk across retail rests on the whims of the Trump Administration as well as the Supreme Court. Recently, the Treasury Secretary mentioned his expectation that a Supreme Court ruling on Learning Resources Inc. v. Trump will come down in January. If the Court sides with retailers, the White House would be forced to remove and/or refund tariffs; the ancillary benefit would go directly to a company like V.F. Corp.

Conclusion

The retail landscape for V.F. Corp. remains cloudy, and I expect the company to post lackluster earnings over the next six to twelve months. We will know much more come January & February, when we find out the results of holiday sales as well as a potential ruling on tariffs. But for the time being, VFC, with its history of mixed merger & acquisition outcomes, stands to lose roughly 15% or more of its value, with the possibility of goodwill impairments to come down the road. Vans will continue to be a drag on earnings, making the “reinvent” turnaround harder to execute. All told, VFC is a short opportunity over the next six to twelve months, as the company works to navigate multiple headwinds coming toward it.

data.title: Why Ichor's 2026 Recovery Is Underestimated
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Multiple catalysts combined, provide evidence that Ichor Holding (ICHR) fundamentals will improve in 2026 and the share price is in the process of bottoming. I am inclined to assign a BUY rating because of the following.

Firstly, last month former CTO, Philip Barros was named CEO, signaling a shift towards operational discipline and innovation.
